title: International trade & exchange-rate systems
summary:
## Why countries trade

Specialisation and trade let countries consume **beyond their own PPC**. The theoretical basis is **comparative advantage**.

- **Absolute advantage** — a country can produce a good using **fewer resources** than another.
- **Comparative advantage (Ricardo)** — a country should specialise in the good in which it has the **lower opportunity cost**, even if it lacks absolute advantage. Mutual gains arise if the **terms of trade** lie between the two countries' opportunity-cost ratios.
- **Assumptions/limitations:** no transport costs, factor immobility between countries, constant returns, ignores externalities and over-dependence — good evaluation material.

## The terms of trade

**Terms of trade = (index of export prices ÷ index of import prices) × 100.** A rise (‘improvement’) means each unit of exports buys more imports. Long-run declines hurt **primary-product exporters** (Prebisch–Singer).

## Gains and costs of free trade

- **Gains:** lower prices and more choice, economies of scale, competition and efficiency, access to resources/technology, higher growth.
- **Costs:** structural unemployment in declining sectors, over-dependence, infant industries exposed, and current-account/environmental concerns.

## Exchange-rate systems

- **Floating** — the rate is set by **demand and supply** of the currency; adjusts automatically, frees monetary policy, but can be **volatile**.
- **Fixed** — the government/central bank **pegs** the rate and defends it using **reserves and interest rates** (**devaluation/revaluation** are policy changes). Gives certainty for trade/investment but requires large reserves and constrains policy.
- **Managed float** — mostly market-determined but with occasional central-bank intervention (the common real-world case).

## Adjustment and evaluation

- A **depreciation/devaluation** should improve the current account, but only if the **Marshall–Lerner condition** holds (PEDx + PEDm > 1). In the short run demand is inelastic, so the trade balance may **worsen first then improve — the J-curve**.
- **Expenditure-switching** (exchange-rate/tariff changes) vs **expenditure-reducing** (deflationary demand policies) approaches to correcting a deficit.

## Egypt application

Egypt moved from a defended peg to a more **flexible/managed EGP**; the resulting **devaluations** improved export and tourism competitiveness but caused **imported inflation** — a live illustration of the fixed-vs-floating trade-off and the J-curve.

## Why it matters

Trade theory and exchange-rate systems connect micro (comparative advantage, specialisation) with macro (AD, inflation, the current account) and set up protectionism and global institutions in Unit 10.

keyTerms.definition: Producing a good with fewer resources than another country.
Producing a good at a lower opportunity cost than another country; the basis for beneficial trade.
The ratio of export prices to import prices (×100); a rise means exports buy more imports.
A rate determined by market demand and supply for the currency.
A rate pegged and defended by the central bank using reserves and interest rates.
A mainly market-determined rate with occasional central-bank intervention.
A depreciation improves the current account only if the price elasticities of exports and imports sum to more than one.
After a depreciation the trade balance worsens before improving, due to short-run inelastic demand.

diagrams: **Comparative advantage:** a two-country, two-good table/PPC showing opportunity costs and the mutually beneficial terms of trade band.
**FOREX market:** demand and supply of a currency setting the floating rate; show intervention/pegging under a fixed system.
**J-curve:** current-account balance over time, dipping after a depreciation then rising above the start.
tips: Define comparative advantage by **lower opportunity cost** (not fewer resources — that's absolute advantage) and show the **terms-of-trade band** for mutual gains.
Always **evaluate free trade's assumptions** (transport costs, immobility, externalities, over-dependence).
Give a **balanced fixed-vs-floating** comparison (certainty & discipline vs flexibility & policy freedom).
State the **Marshall–Lerner condition** and the **J-curve** when analysing a depreciation's effect on the current account.
Use the **EGP** experience as a concrete application of devaluation, competitiveness and imported inflation.

channels.name: EconplusDal
channels.why: Strong on Marshall–Lerner, J-curve and fixed vs floating.
